content.body:
Flame tests (metal cations):
Li⁺ → crimson red
Na⁺ → orange-yellow
K⁺ → lilac/purple
Ca²⁺ → brick red
Ba²⁺ → apple green
Cu²⁺ → blue-green

Anion tests:
Cl⁻: AgNO₃ → white ppt (insoluble in HNO₃)
Br⁻: AgNO₃ → cream ppt
I⁻: AgNO₃ → yellow ppt
SO₄²⁻: BaCl₂ + HCl → white ppt (BaSO₄)
CO₃²⁻: HCl → CO₂ gas (limewater test)
NO₃⁻: Al foil + NaOH → NH₃ gas (brown ring test)

content.title: Cation Tests in Solution
content.body:
NH₄⁺: NaOH → NH₃ gas (turns damp red litmus blue)
Fe²⁺: NaOH → green precipitate
Fe³⁺: NaOH → rust/brown precipitate
Cu²⁺: NaOH → pale blue precipitate
Al³⁺: NaOH → white precipitate (dissolves in excess)
Zn²⁺: NaOH → white precipitate (dissolves in excess NaOH)
Ca²⁺: NaOH → white precipitate (insoluble in excess)

content.title: Systematic Analysis
content.body:
1. Check colour/appearance of substance
2. Flame test on solid
3. Dissolve in water — note colour and any reaction
4. Add NaOH to solution — note precipitate colour
5. Test for anion with specific reagent
6. Confirm results with second test

questions.question: Sodium gives which flame test colour
questions.explanation: Sodium: bright orange-yellow flame. Very distinctive — even tiny amounts give strong orange colour

questions.question: Potassium flame colour is
questions.explanation: K⁺ gives lilac (purple) flame — must be viewed through blue glass to distinguish from Na⁺ interference

questions.question: Fe²⁺ with NaOH gives
questions.explanation: Fe(OH)₂: green/dirty green precipitate. Often oxidises slowly to rusty Fe(OH)₃ on standing

questions.question: Testing for Cl⁻: add AgNO₃ and HNO₃. Result for chloride =
questions.explanation: AgCl: white precipitate, insoluble in dilute HNO₃ (Br⁻=cream, I⁻=yellow)

questions.question: Test for SO₄²⁻ uses
questions.explanation: Ba²⁺ + SO₄²⁻ → BaSO₄ (white precipitate). HCl added first to remove CO₃²⁻ interference

questions.question: NH₄⁺ is identified by
options: NaOH → NH₃ gas turning damp red litmus blue
questions.answer: NaOH → NH₃ gas turning damp red litmus blue
questions.explanation: NH₄⁺ + OH⁻ → NH₃↑ + H₂O. NH₃ is alkaline → blue litmus

questions.question: Al³⁺ and Zn²⁺ both form white precipitates with NaOH. To distinguish: add excess NaOH. Result:
options: Al³⁺ precipitate dissolves (amphoteric), Zn²⁺ precipitate also dissolves
questions.answer: Al³⁺ precipitate dissolves (amphoteric), Zn²⁺ precipitate also dissolves
questions.explanation: Both dissolve in excess NaOH — both amphoteric! Distinguish by other tests (flame: Al no flame, Zn no flame) or other reactions

questions.question: CO₃²⁻ is detected by adding acid and testing evolved gas with
questions.explanation: CO₃²⁻ + 2H⁺ → CO₂↑. CO₂ turns limewater cloudy: Ca(OH)₂ + CO₂ → CaCO₃↓

questions.question: Cu²⁺ ions in solution appear
questions.explanation: Aqueous Cu²⁺ is blue (due to [Cu(H₂O)₆]²⁺ complex ion)

questions.question: Copper flame test colour
questions.explanation: Cu²⁺ gives blue-green (turquoise) flame colour in flame test

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Titration: acid-base reaction to find unknown concentration

Molarity (M) = moles/volume(L)
moles = concentration × volume(L)

At equivalence point:
moles acid = moles base (for 1:1 reaction)
For H₂SO₄ + 2NaOH: moles H₂SO₄ = ½ × moles NaOH

n₁C₁V₁ = n₂C₂V₂
n=stoichiometric coefficient, C=concentration, V=volume

content.title: Titration Calculation
content.body:
25cm³ of 0.1M NaOH neutralised by 20cm³ H₂SO₄.
Moles NaOH = 0.1 × 25/1000 = 0.0025 mol
H₂SO₄ + 2NaOH → Na₂SO₄ + 2H₂O
Moles H₂SO₄ = 0.0025/2 = 0.00125 mol
Concentration H₂SO₄ = 0.00125/(20/1000) = 0.0625 mol/L

content.title: Procedure
content.body:
1. Fill burette with acid (read initial volume)
2. Pipette 25cm³ of alkali into conical flask
3. Add 2-3 drops of indicator (phenolphthalein or methyl orange)
4. Slowly add acid, swirling, until colour just changes (endpoint)
5. Record final burette volume
6. Repeat for concordant results (within 0.1cm³)
7. Calculate average titre

questions.question: Molarity (M) is defined as
questions.explanation: Molarity: M = moles of solute / volume of solution in litres

questions.question: Moles in 250mL of 0.4M HCl
Phenolphthalein endpoint colour change (acid to alkali)
questions.explanation: Phenolphthalein: colourless in acid/neutral, pink/magenta in alkali. Endpoint: colourless → first permanent pink

questions.question: Concordant titres mean
questions.explanation: Concordant results: repeatable to within 0.1cm³, showing consistent technique

questions.question: 30cm³ of 0.2M KOH neutralises HCl. Moles KOH =
H₂SO₄ + 2NaOH → Na₂SO₄ + 2H₂O. Relationship between moles:
questions.explanation: 1 mole H₂SO₄ reacts with 2 moles NaOH

questions.question: Burette is used rather than measuring cylinder because
options: it allows precise volume measurement to 0.1cm³
questions.answer: it allows precise volume measurement to 0.1cm³
questions.explanation: Burette graduated in 0.1cm³ divisions allows accurate, precise volume delivery

questions.question: The purpose of back titration is to
options: determine excess reactant when direct titration is difficult
questions.answer: determine excess reactant when direct titration is difficult
questions.explanation: Back titration: add excess reagent, then titrate the excess — used when direct reaction is slow or impure solid

questions.question: 25cm³ of NaOH neutralised by 12.5cm³ of 0.1M HCl. [NaOH] =
Standard solution is
options: one of accurately KNOWN concentration used as reference
questions.answer: one of accurately KNOWN concentration used as reference
questions.explanation: Standard solution: precisely known concentration, used to calibrate or calculate unknown concentrations in titration

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Chemical Analysis. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Chemical Analysis concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Chemical Analysis builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 3.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Chemical Analysis. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 3.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Chemical Analysis. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Chemical Analysis include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Chemical Analysis. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Chemical Analysis. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Chemical Analysis include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Chemical Analysis. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
